pmos

SQL Fundamentals — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. "How many users signed up last week, and how many came back?" If you have to file a ticket and wait two days for that answer, you are slower than you need to be. SQL (Structured Query Language) is the language you type to ask a database a question and get the rows back yourself. The good news for product managers: you do not need the whole language. Four ideas — pick columns (SELECT), filter rows (WHERE), summarize (GROUP BY with aggregates), and stitch tables together (JOIN) — answer most product questions. This primer is a deep, plain-English tour written for PMs who have never written a query: what a database actually looks like; the SELECT statement; filtering, sorting, counting, grouping, and filtering groups; how tables join; the surprising order SQL really runs in; worked patterns for real product questions; and the traps that quietly produce wrong numbers. Every keyword is shown in a small example and every acronym is spelled out. Source provenance: curation-consensus across the SQL-teaching canon — the analyst tutorial first built by Mode (now ThoughtSpot), W3Schools, the official PostgreSQL documentation, freeCodeCamp, and PM-specific guides from Dept Agency, HelloPM, and others. 18 verified sources.

Why product managers should learn SQL

SQL is, at its plainest, a programming language for asking questions of data. ThoughtSpot's analyst tutorial defines it as a programming language designed for managing data in a relational database — a "relational database" simply being an organized store of data spread across linked tables, which the next section unpacks. You write a question in SQL, the database runs it, and you get a table of answers back.

The reason it matters for a product manager is independence. Dept Agency's primer for PMs puts the promise bluntly: if you can ask a question about your data, you can ask it in SQL — which means you are not waiting on an engineer for every "how many?" HelloPM lists the same payoff as the ability to Make decisions faster by reducing dependency on your data teams for basic analysis and using them for better challenges, plus the chance to uncover insights that are not available with standard tools such as GA, mixpanel etc. (GA is Google Analytics, a common off-the-shelf web-analytics product; Mixpanel is a product-analytics tool — both give you pre-built reports but not arbitrary questions.)

How much do you actually need? Less than you might fear. The AI2SQL guide claims Most product managers can learn enough SQL to run the 10 queries in this guide within one to two weeks of casual practice. The same guide names the short list of ideas that carry the load — read, filter, aggregate, join — which is the spine of this primer.

How a database is organized

Before the verbs, the nouns. A relational database stores data in tables. A table is very much like a spreadsheet tab: rows are records (one user, one order, one event) and columns are fields (a name, a date, an amount). ThoughtSpot's tutorial draws exactly that comparison — tables resemble spreadsheets with rows and columns but follow stricter rules, for instance that each column must have a unique name.

Two special columns make the "relational" part work. A primary key is the column that gives each row a unique identity. W3Schools defines it as the constraint that uniquely identifies each record in a database table — it ensures unique values, and cannot contain NULL values, and a table can have only one. Think of a user_id column in a users table: every user gets exactly one, and no two share it.

A foreign key is how one table points at another. W3Schools: A FOREIGN KEY is a column in a table that refers to the PRIMARY KEY in another table, a link that establishes a link between two tables. An orders table might carry a user_id foreign key so each order knows which user placed it. That single shared column is what lets you later join the two tables back together — the whole reason data is split across tables in the first place.

The SELECT statement

Every query starts with SELECT — the verb for "read." W3Schools states it simply: The SELECT statement is used to select data from a database, written as SELECT column1, column2, ... FROM table_name;. You name the columns you want, then say which table they come from with FROM. So SELECT name, signup_date FROM users; returns just those two columns for every user.

If you want every column, there is a shortcut: To select ALL columns, without specifying every column name, use the SELECT * syntax — the asterisk means "all columns," as in SELECT * FROM users;. It is handy for a first peek at a table, but naming the columns you actually need is the better habit for real analysis.

The official PostgreSQL documentation describes the same statement more formally, as having a select list (the part that lists the columns to be returned), a table list (the part that lists the tables from which to retrieve the data), and an optional qualification (the part that specifies any restrictions). That "optional qualification" is the filter — the WHERE clause — which is the next idea, and the one that turns a data dump into an answer.

Filtering with WHERE

WHERE narrows a query to the rows you care about. W3Schools: The WHERE clause is used to filter records and extract only those records that fulfill a specific condition. The classic shape is SELECT * FROM Customers WHERE Country = 'Mexico'; — give me only the rows where the condition is true.

The conditions are built from comparison operators. W3Schools lists the common ones: equal (=), greater/less than (>, <), not equal (<>), BETWEEN for a range, LIKE for pattern matching (e.g. names starting with "A"), and IN for a list of allowed values. You combine conditions with boolean words. The PostgreSQL docs note that a WHERE clause contains a Boolean (truth value) expression, and only rows for which the Boolean expression is true are returned, and that The usual Boolean operators (AND, OR, and NOT) are allowed.

So WHERE country = 'Mexico' AND signup_date > '2026-01-01' returns Mexican users who joined this year — both conditions must hold. Swap AND for OR and either condition qualifies a row. This is where most of a PM's day-to-day filtering lives: a date window, a country, a plan tier, an event type.

Sorting and limiting results

Once you have the right rows, you usually want them in a useful order. ORDER BY does that. W3Schools: The ORDER BY keyword is used to sort the result-set in ascending or descending order, and by default it sorts the result-set in ascending order (ASC) by default — smallest first, A to Z. To flip it, use the DESC keyword, as in SELECT * FROM Products ORDER BY Price DESC; to see the most expensive first.

You can sort by more than one column, and the order matters: the PostgreSQL docs show ORDER BY city, temp_lo sorts first by city, then breaks ties by the second column. They note that without a fully specified sort you might get the San Francisco rows in either order — a quiet reminder that a database returns rows in no guaranteed order unless you ask.

The companion to sorting is limiting. Most databases let you cap the output (commonly LIMIT 10) so a "top 10 customers by spend" query returns ten rows, not ten thousand. Sort descending, then limit — that two-step is the backbone of every "top N" question a PM asks.

Aggregating with COUNT, SUM, and AVG

So far every query returns rows. Aggregate functions instead return a single summary number computed across many rows — the move from "list the orders" to "how many orders, and worth how much?" ThoughtSpot defines them as functions that perform operations across entire columns (which could include millions of rows of data or more).

The five you will use constantly, in ThoughtSpot's words: COUNT counts how many rows are in a particular column; SUM adds together all the values in a particular column; MIN and MAX return the lowest and highest values in a particular column, respectively; and AVG calculates the average of a group of selected values. So SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users; is your total user count, and SELECT AVG(amount) FROM orders; is your average order value.

One subtlety worth meeting early, because it bites PMs: COUNT has variants. W3Schools notes that The COUNT() function returns the number of rows that matches a specified criterion, but COUNT(DISTINCT column_name) counts only the unique, non-null values in the column. Counting orders and counting distinct customers who ordered are different questions — the pitfalls section returns to this.

Grouping with GROUP BY

An aggregate over a whole table gives you one number. Usually you want one number per category — signups per month, revenue per country, events per feature. GROUP BY is the clause that splits the rows into buckets so an aggregate runs once per bucket. ThoughtSpot: GROUP BY allows you to separate data into groups, which can be aggregated independently of one another.

W3Schools ties it directly to the aggregates: The GROUP BY statement is used to group rows that have the same values into summary rows, like 'Find the number of customers in each country', and it is almost always used in conjunction with aggregate functions, like COUNT(), MAX(), MIN(), SUM(), AVG(), to perform calculations on each group. The canonical shape: SELECT country, COUNT(*) FROM customers GROUP BY country; — one row out per country, with its count.

You can group by several columns at once. ThoughtSpot notes you can group by multiple columns, but you have to separate column names with commas — for example country and month, to see each country's signups broken out by month. The mental rule of thumb: whatever non-aggregated columns you SELECT, you generally also GROUP BY.

Filtering groups with HAVING

Here is a trap with a clean fix. Suppose you want only countries with more than 100 customers. You cannot put that in WHERE, because WHERE runs before the grouping happens — it filters individual rows, and at that point the per-group count does not exist yet. The answer is HAVING.

W3Schools: The HAVING clause is used to filter the results of a GROUP BY query based on aggregate functions. The distinction is the whole point: Unlike the WHERE clause, which filters individual rows before grouping, the HAVING clause filters groups after the aggregation has been performed. So the query becomes ... GROUP BY Country HAVING COUNT(CustomerID) > 5; — group first, count each group, then keep only the groups that clear the bar.

A simple way to hold the two apart: WHERE filters rows; HAVING filters groups. If your condition is about a raw column value (country, date, plan), it goes in WHERE. If it is about an aggregate (a count, a sum, an average per group), it goes in HAVING. The reason WHERE cannot do the second job is purely about timing — which the order-of-execution section makes concrete.

Joining tables together

Real questions span tables. "What did each user spend?" needs the users table (for names) and the orders table (for amounts), connected by the shared key from the structure section. A JOIN does that connecting. freeCodeCamp: The JOIN operator lets you combine related information in various ways — you tell SQL which column the two tables share, and it lines up the matching rows.

The variants differ only in what they do with rows that have no match on the other side. An inner join, in freeCodeCamp's words, will keep only the information from the two joined tables that is related — users who never ordered simply drop out. A LEFT JOIN will keep the unrelated data from the left (the first) table — every user appears, and those with no orders show blank (NULL) where the order data would be. A FULL OUTER JOIN keeps all rows from both tables, and the missing data will be filled in with NULL.

That NULL behavior is not a detail — it is the decision. (NULL is SQL's marker for "no value here," distinct from zero or an empty string.) If you inner-join users to orders and then count users, you silently exclude everyone who never ordered, which understates your user base. If you mean "all users, with their spend where it exists," you want a LEFT JOIN. Choosing the wrong join is one of the most common ways a PM's query returns a confidently wrong number.

The order SQL actually runs

SQL is written SELECT first, but it does not run that way — and that mismatch explains several confusing errors. GeeksforGeeks lays out the logical processing order: FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY. The database first gathers the tables (FROM and any joins), filters rows (WHERE), forms groups (GROUP BY), filters the groups (HAVING), then figures out the columns to return (SELECT), and finally sorts (ORDER BY).

It cautions that this should be understood as a logical processing order, not the actual execution sequence — the real plan is chosen by the query optimizer, which decides the most efficient way to retrieve data. But the logical order is what you reason with, and it pays off immediately.

It explains two things that otherwise look arbitrary. First, why HAVING exists separately from WHERE: WHERE runs before grouping, so it cannot see group-level counts. Second, a naming quirk DataCamp spells out — columns defined with aliases in the SELECT clause cannot be referenced directly in the WHERE clause since it is processed before the SELECT, yet the ORDER BY clause can use column aliases defined in the SELECT statement because ORDER BY runs last. Keep the running order in your head and these stop being mysteries.

Answering product questions with SQL

Put the pieces together and the everyday PM questions fall out as recognizable shapes. AI2SQL's guide names the recurring ones — among them DAU/MAU (Daily and Monthly Active Users), feature-adoption rate, retention by cohort, conversion-funnel analysis, and revenue per user. (DAU, WAU, and MAU are daily, weekly, and monthly active users; ARPU is average revenue per user.)

Most of these are the same four moves in different combinations. A monthly-active-user count is a filter on a date window plus COUNT(DISTINCT user_id). Signups per month is a GROUP BY on the signup month with a COUNT — exactly HelloPM's practice prompt, Number of user registrations every month. "Top features by usage" is a GROUP BY feature, COUNT, ORDER BY that count DESC, LIMIT. Even a quirky question like HelloPM's On which day of the week do you get the most number of users is GROUP BY day-of-week, COUNT, ORDER BY DESC.

The reason this is worth your time, in AI2SQL's framing, is speed: it argues SQL removes that bottleneck so you get answers in minutes instead of days. The deeper queries — multi-step funnels, cohort retention tables — build on these same primitives with joins and a few more functions, and they are where you genuinely benefit from pairing with a data analyst rather than going it alone.

Common pitfalls and what PMs should know

SQL will cheerfully give you a precise, wrong answer, so a few habits matter more than any syntax. The first trap is counting the wrong thing. Because COUNT(DISTINCT column_name) counts only the unique, non-null values in the column, COUNT(*) on an orders table counts orders, not customers — if you want active users, you almost always mean COUNT(DISTINCT user_id). Reporting orders as if they were people is a classic inflated metric.

The second trap is NULL. Recall that a primary key cannot contain NULL values, but plenty of ordinary columns can, and NULL is contagious: it is not zero, it is not equal to anything (not even another NULL), and most aggregates skip it. An average that silently ignores NULL rows, or a filter that unexpectedly drops them, is a frequent source of "the number looks off."

The third is the join trap from earlier — an inner join quietly excluding non-matching rows — and its cousin, the double-counting that happens when a one-to-many join multiplies rows before you aggregate. The practical posture: PMs almost always query a read-only copy of the data, so you cannot break production by selecting; sanity-check a new query against a number you already trust; and when a result drives a real decision, have a data analyst review the query. Dept Agency's framing is the right ambition — if you can ask a question about your data, you can ask it in SQL — held together with the humility to verify before you act on it.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

SQL is the entry point to a wider analytics toolkit. These are pointers, not lessons — each is its own study.

  • Product analytics — the event model (users, actions, properties) that your queries run against; SQL is how you interrogate the raw events behind a Mixpanel or Amplitude dashboard.
  • Cohort analysis and retention — the retention table and curve are built from GROUP BY plus joins over signup-date cohorts; SQL is the construction tool.
  • Funnel and conversion analysis — multi-step funnels chain filtered counts together, the next rung up from single-query aggregation.
  • Window functions — the more advanced SQL (running totals, rankings, period-over-period) that picks up where GROUP BY leaves off.
  • Dashboards and business-intelligence tools — once a query is trustworthy, tools like Looker, Metabase, or Tableau turn it into a reusable, shareable chart.

References

  1. thoughtspot.com ·cited· (T1)

    The widely used analyst SQL tutorial (originally Mode's, now ThoughtSpot's) defines it plainly: "SQL (Structured Query Language) is a programming language designed for managing data in a relational database." It notes analysts can access large datasets directly without copying them elsewhere, and that SQL analysis is "readily auditable and reproducible" — tables work like spreadsheets with rows and columns but with stricter rules, e.g. "each column must have a unique name."

  2. postgresql.org ·cited· (T1)

    PostgreSQL's official tutorial describes a SELECT as three parts — "a select list (the part that lists the columns to be returned), a table list ... and an optional qualification." A query is "qualified" by a WHERE clause containing a Boolean expression, and "The usual Boolean operators (AND, OR, and NOT) are allowed," with results sorted via ORDER BY (e.g. ORDER BY city, temp_lo).

  3. thoughtspot.com ·cited· (T1)

    ThoughtSpot's tutorial: "Aggregate functions are used to perform operations across entire columns (which could include millions of rows of data or more)." COUNT "counts how many rows are in a particular column," SUM "adds together all the values," MIN and MAX "return the lowest and highest values," and AVG "calculates the average of a group of selected values."

  4. thoughtspot.com ·cited· (T1)

    ThoughtSpot: "GROUP BY allows you to separate data into groups, which can be aggregated independently of one another." You can "group by multiple columns, but you have to separate column names with commas — just as with ORDER BY," and the order of grouping columns does not change the result; use ORDER BY separately to control how grouped results appear.

  5. engineering.deptagency.com ·cited· (T2)

    Dept Agency's SQL 101 for PMs argues self-sufficiency is the whole point: "if you can ask a question about your data, you can ask it in SQL," so PMs are not dependent on engineers for every data inquiry. It frames the relational model as "this idea of a join ... allow different tables to be related to one another via one column."

  6. hellopm.co ·cited· (T2)

    HelloPM's guide gives three reasons PMs learn SQL: an "In-depth understanding of your data," the "Ability to uncover insights that are not available with standard tools such as GA, mixpanel etc," and to "Make decisions faster by reducing dependency on your data teams." It lists practice questions like "Number of user registrations every month" and "On which day of the week do you get the most number of users."

  7. builder.ai2sql.io ·cited· (T2)

    AI2SQL's PM guide reduces the field to four ideas — "The core concepts are SELECT (read data), WHERE (filter), GROUP BY (aggregate), and JOIN (combine tables). Once you understand those four, you can answer most product analytics questions yourself." It estimates "Most product managers can learn enough SQL to run the 10 queries in this guide within one to two weeks of casual practice," the queries being DAU/MAU, feature adoption, retention by cohort, funnel, ARPU, churn, and more.

  8. w3schools.com ·cited· (T2)

    W3Schools defines the workhorse statement: "The SELECT statement is used to select data from a database," written as "SELECT column1, column2, ... FROM table_name;". To pull every column you "use the SELECT * syntax" — e.g. SELECT * FROM Customers; — while naming columns (SELECT CustomerName, City FROM Customers;) returns only those.

  9. w3schools.com ·cited· (T2)

    W3Schools: "The WHERE clause is used to filter records" and "extract only those records that fulfill a specific condition," as in SELECT * FROM Customers WHERE Country = 'Mexico';. It supports operators including =, >, <, >=, <=, <> (not equal), BETWEEN, LIKE (pattern matching), and IN (multiple possible values).

  10. w3schools.com ·cited· (T2)

    W3Schools: "The ORDER BY keyword is used to sort the result-set in ascending or descending order," and it "sorts the result-set in ascending order (ASC) by default." To reverse it, "use the DESC keyword" — e.g. SELECT * FROM Products ORDER BY Price DESC;.

  11. w3schools.com ·cited· (T2)

    W3Schools: "The COUNT() function returns the number of rows that matches a specified criterion." Critically for accurate metrics, "COUNT(DISTINCT column_name) counts only the unique, non-null values in the column" — so COUNT(Price) counts every entry while COUNT(DISTINCT Price) answers "How many different prices are there?"

  12. w3schools.com ·cited· (T2)

    W3Schools: "The GROUP BY statement is used to group rows that have the same values into summary rows, like 'Find the number of customers in each country'." It is "almost always used in conjunction with aggregate functions, like COUNT(), MAX(), MIN(), SUM(), AVG(), to perform calculations on each group."

  13. w3schools.com ·cited· (T2)

    W3Schools: "The HAVING clause is used to filter the results of a GROUP BY query based on aggregate functions." The distinction that trips people up: "Unlike the WHERE clause, which filters individual rows before grouping, the HAVING clause filters groups after the aggregation has been performed" — e.g. GROUP BY Country HAVING COUNT(CustomerID) > 5.

  14. freecodecamp.org ·cited· (T2)

    freeCodeCamp: "The JOIN operator lets you combine related information in various ways." An inner join "will keep only the information from the two joined tables that is related"; a LEFT JOIN "will keep the unrelated data from the left (the first) table"; a FULL OUTER JOIN keeps "all rows from both tables, and the missing data will be filled in with NULL."

  15. w3schools.com ·cited· (T2)

    W3Schools: "The PRIMARY KEY constraint uniquely identifies each record in a database table." It "ensures unique values, and cannot contain NULL values," and "A table can have only ONE PRIMARY KEY constraint" — the column that gives every row a stable, one-of-a-kind identity.

  16. w3schools.com ·cited· (T2)

    W3Schools: "The FOREIGN KEY constraint establishes a link between two tables." Concretely, "A FOREIGN KEY is a column in a table that refers to the PRIMARY KEY in another table" — the mechanism that connects, say, an orders table to the customers table, and the basis for joins.

  17. geeksforgeeks.org ·cited· (T2)

    GeeksforGeeks lays out the logical processing order — FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, SELECT, ORDER BY — and stresses it "should be understood as a logical processing order, not the actual execution sequence," since the real plan is chosen by "the query optimizer, which decides the most efficient way to retrieve data." Filtering and grouping happen before the costly SELECT and sort.

  18. datacamp.com ·cited· (T2)

    DataCamp explains the practical consequence of execution order: "columns defined with aliases in the SELECT clause cannot be referenced directly in the WHERE clause since it is processed before the SELECT." By contrast, "the ORDER BY clause can use column aliases defined in the SELECT statement," because ORDER BY runs after SELECT.

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_sql-fundamentals.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
